hinge: The hamular notch is a visible landmark in both maxillary and mandibular impressions — it is the depression distal to the maxillary tuberosity marking the posterior border of the maxillary denture. Pterygomandibular raphe is a soft tissue landmark not directly impressed.

A patient with all upper teeth and only lingually tilted lower anteriors requires a swing-lock RPD, which uses a hinged labial bar to engage labial undercuts on teeth that have no usable buccal undercuts due to lingual inclination, allowing retention without altering the natural teeth.

Locator attachments are currently the most common attachment system for mandibular implant overdentures with 2 implants due to their self-aligning capability, ease of use, low profile, and excellent retention with various retention inserts to customize force levels.

A tooth with a cervical undercut is best managed with a ring clasp that encircles the entire tooth and engages the undercut with a wrought wire or retentive arm, bypassing the cervical constriction. Aker and RPI clasps rely on mid-buccal undercuts and are contraindicated.

After a survey crown is placed and the tooth no longer requires additional treatment, only contouring adjustments may be needed to ensure proper parallelism and guide planes for the RPD framework — not a full coverage restoration.

A complete denture that dislodges when the patient pulls their lip is caused by the buccal frenum — inadequate frenum relief causes the denture flange to be displaced during muscle contraction of the buccinator and associated frenal pull. Labial frenum affects the anterior midline.

A broken I-bar clasp on a metal base RPD should be conservatively repaired by recasting just the bar clasp and soldering or lasering it to the existing framework, rather than making an entirely new framework. Wrought wire is for replacing circumferential arms, not I-bars.

A severe undercut (>0.01 inch / 0.25 mm) is an absolute contraindication for an I-bar clasp because the rigid approach arm would need excessive blockout or would permanently deform during insertion, losing retention. I-bar clasps are designed for 0.01 inch undercuts.

High posterior cusps require increased vertical overlap of anterior teeth to provide steep anterior guidance that discludes the posteriors in excursive movements and accommodates the cusp height during function. Shallow anterior guidance would require shorter/flatter posterior cusps.

Pinpoint red dots (petechiae) under a denture in a controlled diabetic patient is classic for denture stomatitis caused by Candida. Antifungal treatment is the primary management, usually with topical nystatin or miconazole applied to the fitting surface.

For a recently completed denture with poor retention due to a flat ridge (assuming all other factors are correct), adhesive is the most conservative initial management to improve retention. Reline/rebasing would be considered for significant ridge changes.

Discomfort while wearing an upper RPD in a diabetic patient is most commonly due to pressure areas from the fitting surface of the prosthesis. Reduction from the fitting surface (internal adjustment) at the pressure point provides immediate relief.

For Kennedy Class I (distal extension) with an undercut on the last abutment, the most flexible clasp is wrought wire with round cross-section. Wrought wire is more flexible than cast clasps due to its fibrous grain structure, and round cross-section provides multi-directional flexibility.

Increased vertical dimension reduces the interarch space at rest, causing the corners of the mouth to be compressed, leading to angular cheilitis from saliva pooling and Candida/fungal overgrowth in the moist skin folds at the commissures.

A gunshot wound causing significant bone and tooth loss in quadrant four typically requires a fixed hybrid denture (implant-supported fixed complete denture/hybrid prosthesis) to restore both hard and soft tissue defects while providing stability and function. This requires the image for definitive assessment.

A well-fitting denture that becomes difficult to insert after being left out overnight is due to the denture dehydrating and warping when left in a dry environment, causing dimensional change and tightness upon reinsertion the next morning.

For a Kennedy Class III mandibular RPD with a mid-buccal undercut, a circumferential (Akers) clasp is the clasp of choice, engaging the buccal undercut directly. RPI uses a mesial rest and distobuccal undercut; reverse circumferential is for when undercut is on the opposite side.

Denture base materials are commonly made of chrome-cobalt alloy or palladium-silver alloys. Among the listed options, "palladium" is the most specific metal used in denture frameworks, typically as palladium-silver alloy. This item is ambiguously worded.

Only fully adjustable articulators can capture and reproduce mandibular border movements, as they have adjustable condylar paths, Bennett angle, and intercondylar distance settings. Semi-adjustable articulators approximate but do not fully replicate border movements.

A fractured retentive arm with an intact reciprocal arm can be conservatively repaired by adding a wrought wire retentive clasp soldered or laser-welded to the framework, avoiding the need for a complete remake. Solder is for joining pieces, not replacing a clasp arm.

Missing 4 upper anterior teeth and left upper second premolar = 5 missing teeth. The ideal design places rest seats on each abutment tooth adjacent to edentulous spaces. 6 rest seats with 5 retentive arms distributes forces optimally across the arch while avoiding unnecessary retentive arms on teeth that only need reciprocal support.

Acrylic is packed into the mold during the dough stage — when the monomer and polymer have reached a consistency that is pliable, non-sticky, and workable. Packing at the rubbery stage causes voids; sticky stage adheres to packing materials; stiff stage cannot flow into the mold.

Round cross-section wrought wire provides the greatest flexibility and is most resistant to permanent deformation (dislodgement), allowing it to flex over the height of contour without distorting. Half-round and square cross-sections are stiffer and more prone to permanent deformation.

RPD stability (resistance to lateral forces) is provided by the reciprocal arm (bracing component) and minor connectors (proximal plates/guide planes), which resist horizontal movement. The retentive arm provides retention against vertical displacement; the rest provides support.

For Kennedy Class I (distal extension) where the undercut is not cervical and infrabulge is contraindicated, a mesial rest with distal circumferential clasp (RPA concept) is the appropriate design. Mesial rest provides favorable force distribution; distal retentive arm engages the distal undercut.

A locator attachment is the component that is attached to the denture (the female housing in the denture base) and snaps onto the implant abutment (male portion). Implant is the fixture in bone; analog is the lab replica; coping is a temporary or impression component.

Interference when closing into the optimum condylar position in the glenoid fossa describes a centric interference — a premature contact between centric relation and maximum intercuspation that causes mandibular deflection during closure into CR.

Missing 4 upper anterior teeth with severe ridge resorption creates a challenging esthetic prosthetic scenario. An implant-supported fixed prosthesis is the best option to restore both the missing teeth and lost ridge volume, though it may require grafting. Among the limited options, implant is the most esthetic.

A varying, irregular survey line during cast surveying indicates a dull or blunted carbon marker that needs resharpening — it does not mark a crisp, consistent line on the cast surface. Poor quality carbon or wrong angulation would produce consistently poor lines, not variable width/position.

The posterior palatal seal improves maxillary denture retention by creating a peripheral seal at the junction of the hard and soft palate, preventing air ingress under the denture base and maintaining the negative pressure/suction needed for retention, particularly during function.
